sets.title: Campus Sustainability Program
sets.descriptionZh: 围绕校园可持续发展项目设计的新版阅读短练，覆盖补全单词、校园生活短文和学术型说明文。

sections.title: Complete the Words: Green Campus Notice
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读短文。每个空只输入单词缺失的字母，不要重复已经给出的开头字母。
sections.passage: The university is {{rs001_cw_b01}} a {{rs001_cw_b02}} program that asks students to {{rs001_cw_b03}} waste, {{rs001_cw_b04}} energy, and report {{rs001_cw_b05}} problems. Student {{rs001_cw_b06}} will receive weekly {{rs001_cw_b07}} comparing residence halls. The committee hopes that small, {{rs001_cw_b08}} choices will create a {{rs001_cw_b09}} effect across {{rs001_cw_b10}}.

blanks.explanationZh: expand 表示扩大项目，句中 is expanding 是现在进行时。
sustainability program 表示可持续发展项目，是校园环保语境中的固定表达。
reduce waste 表示减少浪费，符合环保项目目标。
conserve energy 表示节约能源，和 reduce waste 并列。
maintenance problems 指维修问题，影响资源使用。
student volunteers 指学生志愿者，后文说明他们会收到报告。
weekly reports 指每周报告，用来比较宿舍楼。
daily choices 表示日常选择，和 small 并列修饰 choices。
cumulative effect 表示累积效应，符合小选择逐渐产生影响。
across campus 表示在整个校园范围内。

sections.title: Daily Life: Residence Hall Composting
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读校园生活场景短文，选择最佳答案。
sections.passage: Maya finds a note from her residence assistant about a new composting station in the lobby. The note says students should place fruit peels, coffee grounds, and uncoated paper napkins in the green bin, but they should not include meat, plastic forks, or glossy takeout boxes. A volunteer will be in the lobby on Monday evening to answer questions. The note adds that the station is a pilot project; if contamination stays low for six weeks, the university will add bins to two more residence halls.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of the note?
options: To explain how students should use a new composting station
To announce that all dining halls will stop serving meat
To ask students to volunteer for a city recycling center
To compare several residence halls' waste levels
questions.explanationZh: 短文主要说明新堆肥站能放什么、不能放什么以及试点条件。B 夸大为食堂停止供肉，文中只是说肉不能进堆肥桶。
questions.evidence: new composting station; should place; should not include

questions.prompt: According to the note, which item belongs in the green bin?
options: Coffee grounds
Plastic forks
Glossy takeout boxes
Meat scraps
questions.explanationZh: 文中明确列出 coffee grounds 可以放入绿色桶。plastic forks、glossy takeout boxes 和 meat 都被列为不应放入的物品。
questions.evidence: place fruit peels, coffee grounds, and uncoated paper napkins in the green bin

questions.prompt: Why will a volunteer be in the lobby on Monday evening?
options: To answer students' questions
To collect room keys
To sell reusable containers
To inspect every student's trash
questions.explanationZh: 志愿者在大厅的目的就是 answer questions。D 把“回答问题”误解成逐一检查垃圾，文中没有这种要求。
questions.evidence: A volunteer will be in the lobby on Monday evening to answer questions.

sections.title: Academic Passage: Behavior and Campus Energy Use
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术型短文，回答主旨、细节、推断、词义和修辞目的题。
sections.passage: Universities often begin sustainability efforts by purchasing efficient equipment, yet equipment alone rarely produces the largest possible savings. Buildings are used by people whose routines determine when lights, computers, and heating systems operate. For this reason, many campuses now combine technical upgrades with behavioral programs. One common strategy is feedback: residence halls receive weekly reports showing electricity use compared with similar buildings. The comparison is not meant to shame students; rather, it makes an invisible resource visible. When occupants see that a neighboring hall used less energy during the same cold week, they may look for practical explanations, such as open windows or unused devices left on overnight. However, feedback must be designed carefully. If a report only praises the most efficient hall, students in other buildings may feel the goal is unreachable. Successful programs therefore emphasize specific actions and short time frames. A message such as 'turn off desk lamps before breakfast this week' is easier to act on than a broad request to 'save the planet.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Campus energy savings improve when technology is paired with behavior-focused feedback
Universities should avoid buying efficient equipment for residence halls
Students generally refuse to participate in sustainability programs
Heating systems are the only major cause of campus energy waste
questions.explanationZh: 全文强调设备升级之外，还要通过反馈改变使用习惯。B 说不应购买设备，和第一句不符；D 把问题缩小到供暖，过窄。
questions.evidence: combine technical upgrades with behavioral programs

questions.prompt: The word 'occupants' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: people who use a building
machines that measure heat
official campus rules
reports sent by managers
questions.explanationZh: occupants 指建筑中的使用者或居住者，对应前文 buildings are used by people。B 把词误解成设备，不符合句子主语。
questions.evidence: Buildings are used by people... When occupants see

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention a neighboring hall that used less energy?
options: To illustrate how comparison can prompt students to examine their routines
To prove that old buildings can never be efficient
To argue that cold weather has no effect on electricity use
To show that weekly reports are illegal in some dormitories
questions.explanationZh: 相邻宿舍的比较让学生寻找开放窗户、设备未关等实际原因，说明反馈如何引发行为检查。B 和 D 没有依据；C 还与 cold week 的背景不符。
questions.evidence: they may look for practical explanations, such as open windows

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what is a risk of praising only the most efficient hall?
options: Other students may think improvement is impossible
The efficient hall will stop receiving electricity
Reports will become too short to read
The university will cancel all technical upgrades
questions.explanationZh: 原文说其他楼学生可能觉得目标 unreachable。B、C、D 都错在是原文没有提到的夸张后果。
questions.evidence: students in other buildings may feel the goal is unreachable

questions.prompt: Which recommendation is most consistent with the passage?
options: Give students concrete weekly actions connected to energy reports
Send one general environmental slogan at the start of the year
Hide building-level energy data to avoid competition
Focus only on replacing equipment and ignore routines
questions.explanationZh: 结尾明确支持具体行动和短时间框架。B 的 broad request 正是作者认为不如具体提示有效；D 忽视行为，与全文主旨相反。
questions.evidence: emphasize specific actions and short time frames

sets.title: Library Research Support
sets.descriptionZh: 围绕大学图书馆研究支持服务设计，训练补全单词、校园公告理解和学术信息素养阅读。

sections.title: Complete the Words: Research Help Desk
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读短文。每个空只输入单词缺失的字母，不要重复已经给出的开头字母。
sections.passage: The library research desk helps students {{rs002_cw_b01}} sources, {{rs002_cw_b02}} information, and organize {{rs002_cw_b03}} for class projects. A librarian may suggest {{rs002_cw_b04}} keywords, show how to use {{rs002_cw_b05}} filters, and explain why some websites are less {{rs002_cw_b06}}. Students should keep a {{rs002_cw_b07}} record of search terms so they can {{rs002_cw_b08}} a successful search and {{rs002_cw_b09}} sources before writing a final {{rs002_cw_b10}}.

blanks.explanationZh: locate sources 表示找到资料来源，是图书馆研究支持的核心动作。
evaluate information 表示评估信息可靠性。
organize citations 表示整理引用信息，符合 class projects 语境。
alternative keywords 指可替换关键词，帮助缩小或调整搜索。
database filters 指数据库筛选器。
less reliable 指不太可靠的网站，符合评估信息质量。
systematic record 表示有系统的记录，方便复现搜索。
repeat a successful search 表示重复一次有效搜索。
compare sources 表示比较资料来源。
final report 指最终报告，是 class project 的常见产出。

sections.title: Daily Life: Booking a Librarian
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读校园生活场景短文，选择最佳答案。
sections.passage: Omar needs sources for a sociology presentation, but the library website lists hundreds of database results. He opens the research support page and sees that students can book a twenty-minute appointment with a subject librarian. The appointment can be online or at the reference desk. Students are asked to upload their assignment prompt and two keywords before the meeting, so the librarian can prepare examples. Same-day appointments are sometimes available, but the page recommends booking two days ahead during midterm week.

questions.prompt: Why is Omar likely using the research support page?
options: He needs help narrowing database results for a presentation
He wants to reserve a group study room for a party
He is applying for a job at the library
He needs to pay a fine for an overdue book
questions.explanationZh: Omar 的问题是数据库结果太多且要做 sociology presentation，因此预约学科馆员最合理。B、C、D 都不是短文中的需求。
questions.evidence: needs sources for a sociology presentation; hundreds of database results

questions.prompt: What must students upload before the meeting?
options: The assignment prompt and two keywords
A complete bibliography and a photo ID
Their class schedule and dorm address
A recording of their presentation
questions.explanationZh: 短文明确说上传 assignment prompt and two keywords。B 错在 bibliography 可能和研究有关但文中没有要求。
questions.evidence: upload their assignment prompt and two keywords before the meeting

questions.prompt: Why does the page recommend booking two days ahead during midterm week?
options: Appointments may be harder to get at that busy time
The library closes for the entire week
Librarians refuse to meet online during exams
Students need two days to receive a library card
questions.explanationZh: 期中周需求高，所以建议提前两天预约。B、C、D 都错在没有文本依据，也过于绝对。
questions.evidence: Same-day appointments are sometimes available, but... booking two days ahead during midterm week

sections.title: Academic Passage: Search Strategies in Digital Libraries
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术型短文，回答主旨、细节、推断、词义和修辞目的题。
sections.passage: Digital libraries have made scholarly material easier to reach, but easy access does not always produce effective research. A student who types a broad phrase into a search box may receive thousands of results, many only loosely connected to the topic. Librarians therefore teach search as an iterative process. Researchers begin with a tentative question, test several keywords, examine the language used in promising articles, and then revise the search. This cycle is especially useful because academic fields often use specialized terms. For example, articles about city parks may discuss 'urban green space,' while a student may initially search only for 'parks.' Another important strategy is filtering. Date limits, peer-review options, and subject categories can remove irrelevant material, but filters should not be applied mechanically. A narrow date range may exclude a classic study that later research continues to cite. Effective searching is thus neither a single click nor a random exploration; it is a controlled process of adjustment.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why effective digital research requires repeated adjustment of searches
Why students should avoid using online library databases
How librarians decide which books to buy each year
How city parks are designed in modern urban planning
questions.explanationZh: 全文围绕 iterative process、关键词修订和过滤器调整，说明数字研究需要反复调整。D 只是例子中的内容，不是主旨。
questions.evidence: search as an iterative process; controlled process of adjustment

questions.prompt: The word 'tentative' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: preliminary
permanent
unrelated
illegal
questions.explanationZh: tentative question 指初步问题，之后会测试和修改。B permanent 错在与后文 revise the search 的过程相反。
questions.evidence: begin with a tentative question... then revise the search

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention 'urban green space'?
options: To show that scholarly articles may use terms students do not initially search for
To argue that parks are not appropriate research topics
To explain why databases contain no articles about cities
To recommend replacing all keywords with longer phrases
questions.explanationZh: 这个例子说明学术领域常用专门术语，学生需要从相关文章中发现并调整关键词。D 过度推断，并不是所有关键词都要变长。
questions.evidence: academic fields often use specialized terms

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what is a possible problem with a narrow date filter?
options: It may exclude an older study that remains influential
It always increases the number of irrelevant results
It prevents students from seeing peer-reviewed articles
It changes article titles into unfamiliar terms
questions.explanationZh: 原文指出窄日期范围可能排除仍被引用的经典研究。B 的 always 绝对化，且与过滤器通常减少结果不符。
questions.evidence: A narrow date range may exclude a classic study that later research continues to cite.

questions.prompt: Which statement best reflects the author's view of filters?
options: They are useful when applied thoughtfully
They should replace keyword revision entirely
They are harmful in every scholarly search
They matter only for printed books
questions.explanationZh: 作者承认过滤器能去除无关材料，但提醒不要机械使用，所以应“谨慎、有思考地使用”。C 说总是有害，和前半句矛盾。
questions.evidence: filters can remove irrelevant material, but filters should not be applied mechanically

sets.title: Intro Biology Lab Safety
sets.descriptionZh: 围绕入门生物实验室安全主题设计，包含补全单词、实验室通知和学术说明文。

sections.title: Complete the Words: Lab Safety Orientation
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读短文。每个空只输入单词缺失的字母，不要重复已经给出的开头字母。
sections.passage: Introductory biology students must learn basic {{rs003_cw_b01}} routines before handling {{rs003_cw_b02}}, stains, glassware, or electrical {{rs003_cw_b03}}. Goggles are required when liquid may {{rs003_cw_b04}}, and broken glass belongs in a {{rs003_cw_b05}} container. The instructor will {{rs003_cw_b06}} each procedure, keep labels {{rs003_cw_b07}}, and ask students to report spills {{rs003_cw_b08}}. These habits reduce {{rs003_cw_b09}} and support careful {{rs003_cw_b10}}.

blanks.explanationZh: safety routines 指安全流程，是实验室主题核心。
specimens 指实验样本，和 stains、glassware 并列。
electrical equipment 指电气设备，实验室安全中常见。
liquid may splash 表示液体可能飞溅，因此需要护目镜。
designated container 表示指定容器，符合碎玻璃处理规则。
instructor will demonstrate 表示教师会演示步骤。
labels legible 表示标签清晰可读。
report spills immediately 表示溢洒后立即报告。
reduce uncertainty 表示减少不确定性，呼应安全习惯的作用。
careful observation 指细致观察，是实验课目标。

sections.title: Daily Life: First Lab Session
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读校园生活场景短文，选择最佳答案。
sections.passage: Before her first biology lab, Lina reads a message from the teaching assistant. Students should arrive ten minutes early to store backpacks along the side wall, put on closed-toe shoes, and review the safety quiz. Food and drinks are not allowed at the benches. The message also says that anyone who missed the online safety quiz may still attend class, but must complete the quiz on a lab computer before touching any materials. Extra goggles are available near the entrance.

questions.prompt: What should students do with backpacks?
options: Store them along the side wall
Keep them under the lab bench
Hang them on microscope handles
Leave them outside the building
questions.explanationZh: 信息明确要求把背包放在 side wall。B 错在文中没有说放桌下，且实验室安全通常避免堵塞工作区。
questions.evidence: store backpacks along the side wall

questions.prompt: What happens if a student missed the online safety quiz?
options: The student must finish it before handling materials
The student is permanently removed from the course
The student may skip goggles for that class
The student must bring food for the group
questions.explanationZh: 未完成线上安全测验的人仍可上课，但接触材料前必须在实验室电脑完成。B 错在 permanently removed 过度夸张。
questions.evidence: must complete the quiz on a lab computer before touching any materials

questions.prompt: Why should students arrive ten minutes early?
options: To complete preparation steps before lab work begins
To watch a film unrelated to the course
To clean the entire laboratory alone
To meet in the cafeteria before class
questions.explanationZh: 提前到达是为了存包、穿合适鞋子、复习安全测验等准备事项。C 错在把准备工作夸大成独自清洁整个实验室，文中没有依据。
questions.evidence: arrive ten minutes early to store backpacks... put on closed-toe shoes, and review the safety quiz

sections.title: Academic Passage: Why Introductory Labs Emphasize Safety
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术型短文，回答主旨、细节、推断、词义和修辞目的题。
sections.passage: Safety instruction in introductory biology laboratories is sometimes mistaken for a set of arbitrary rules. In fact, these routines reflect the way risk is managed in scientific work. Many first-year experiments use familiar materials, such as plant tissues or food coloring, but familiarity can create false confidence. A student who casually carries a beaker across a crowded room may not consider that wet glass is slippery or that a small spill can damage electrical equipment. Safety rules therefore aim to make hazards visible before an accident occurs. Personal protective equipment is only one part of this system. Clear labels, uncluttered benches, and immediate reporting of spills all reduce uncertainty. The goal is not to make students fearful; it is to help them develop habits that allow careful observation and collaboration. When safety becomes routine, students spend less attention wondering what to do in an emergency and more attention on the biological question being tested.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Lab safety routines help students manage risk and focus on scientific work
Introductory biology labs should avoid all familiar materials
Personal protective equipment is unnecessary in first-year courses
Most accidents are caused by food coloring
questions.explanationZh: 文章说明安全规则并非任意规定，而是风险管理和形成科研习惯的方式。B 误解 familiar materials 的例子；D 把一个材料例子当成事故主因。
questions.evidence: routines reflect the way risk is managed; develop habits

questions.prompt: The word 'arbitrary' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: based on no clear reason
carefully tested
required by electricity
easy to observe
questions.explanationZh: arbitrary rules 指看似没有明确理由的规定；后文 in fact 转折说明其实有风险管理原因。B 与转折前的 mistaken view 不一致。
questions.evidence: mistaken for a set of arbitrary rules. In fact, these routines reflect... risk

questions.prompt: Why does the author discuss a student carrying a beaker across a crowded room?
options: To illustrate how ordinary actions can involve overlooked hazards
To prove that beakers should never be used in biology
To suggest that crowded rooms improve collaboration
To show that electrical equipment is always wet
questions.explanationZh: 烧杯例子说明熟悉材料会带来虚假自信，普通动作也可能有被忽视的危险。B 错在 never 过度绝对，文中只是说明风险。
questions.evidence: familiarity can create false confidence; may not consider that wet glass is slippery

questions.prompt: According to the passage, which practice reduces uncertainty?
options: Keeping labels clear
Leaving benches crowded
Reporting spills at the end of the semester
Avoiding collaboration during experiments
questions.explanationZh: 文章列出 clear labels、整洁台面和立即报告溢洒都能减少不确定性。B 与 uncluttered benches 相反；C 与 immediate reporting 相反。
questions.evidence: Clear labels, uncluttered benches, and immediate reporting of spills all reduce uncertainty.

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about well-practiced safety routines?
options: They free students' attention for the experiment's scientific question
They eliminate the need for instructors in the laboratory
They make students more fearful of ordinary materials
They replace the need to observe biological results
questions.explanationZh: 结尾说安全成为常规后，学生能把更多注意力放在被测试的生物学问题上。C 错在与 The goal is not to make students fearful 相反。
questions.evidence: more attention on the biological question being tested

sets.title: Academic Advising Desk
sets.descriptionZh: 围绕学术咨询台、预约和毕业要求的短练，适合巩固校园服务类阅读。

sections.title: Complete the Words: Advising Office Update
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读短文。每个空只输入单词缺失的字母，不要重复已经给出的开头字母。
sections.passage: The advising office is {{rs004_cw_b01}} extra {{rs004_cw_b02}} before registration week. Students can ask about {{rs004_cw_b03}} credit, degree {{rs004_cw_b04}}, and important {{rs004_cw_b05}}. Some changes need {{rs004_cw_b06}} approval, so students should bring all required {{rs004_cw_b07}}. The office asks everyone to {{rs004_cw_b08}} an appointment and check {{rs004_cw_b09}} hours because several {{rs004_cw_b10}} are also leading orientation meetings.

blanks.explanationZh: scheduling 表示安排预约，符合 advising desk 的服务内容。
appointments 指预约，和 advising desk 语境一致。
transfer credit 指转学分，是学术咨询常见事项。
degree requirements 指毕业要求，需要复数形式。
deadlines 指截止日期，和 registration 并列。
documents 指需要带来的材料。
confirm an appointment 表示确认预约。
advisers 指学业顾问，复数和 several 搭配。

sections.title: Daily Life: Advising Desk Location
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读校园生活短文，选择最符合原文的信息。
sections.passage: The advising desk has moved from the first floor of Parker Hall to Room 214 in the student services building. Students who only need a form signed can visit during walk-in hours on Tuesday morning. Students with questions about changing majors should schedule a full appointment online because those meetings usually take longer.

questions.prompt: Where is the advising desk now located?
options: On the first floor of Parker Hall
In Room 214 of the student services building
Inside the library reference office
Next to the biology laboratory
questions.explanationZh: 短文第一句直接说明咨询台搬到了 student services building 的 214 室。

questions.prompt: Who should schedule a full appointment online?
options: Students who need a form signed quickly
Students looking for Tuesday walk-in hours
Students with questions about changing majors
Students attending orientation meetings
questions.explanationZh: changing majors 的问题通常更长，所以需要线上预约完整会面。
questions.evidence: changing majors should schedule a full appointment

questions.prompt: Why does the notice mention meeting length?
options: To explain why some students need appointments
To announce a new advising fee
To compare two academic buildings
To cancel Tuesday walk-in hours
questions.explanationZh: 文章用 meeting length 解释为什么换专业咨询不能只走 walk-in。
questions.evidence: because those meetings usually take longer

sections.title: Academic Passage: Course Sequencing
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术说明文，回答主旨、推断、细节和结构题。
sections.passage: Course sequencing can shape how students understand a major. In many programs, introductory classes are not simply easier versions of advanced classes. They introduce methods, vocabulary, and habits of analysis that later courses assume. If students skip these foundations, they may know isolated facts but miss the organizing principles behind them. For example, an economics student might memorize terms from a policy course without understanding the models introduced in a first-year class. Sequencing also helps departments balance workload. A writing-intensive seminar may be scheduled after a research methods course so that students can apply citation and evidence skills. However, rigid sequences can create problems when students change majors or transfer from another institution. Departments therefore often allow substitutions, but they review them carefully. The goal is not to block progress; it is to make sure that students enter advanced courses with the preparation needed to participate fully.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Course sequences help students build preparation for later work.
Introductory courses should be removed from most majors.
Transfer students rarely need academic advising.
Advanced seminars are mainly designed to reduce workload.
questions.explanationZh: 全文说明课程顺序如何建立方法、词汇、分析习惯和后续课程准备。
questions.evidence: shape how students understand a major

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention an economics student?
options: To give an example of missing foundations
To argue that economics is harder than writing
To describe a transfer credit policy
To criticize policy courses in general
questions.explanationZh: 例子说明跳过基础课程会导致知道零散事实但不懂背后的模型。

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why might a seminar follow a methods course?
options: So students can avoid citation work
So students can apply research skills
So departments can cancel substitutions
So transfer students can skip writing
questions.explanationZh: 文中说 writing-intensive seminar 放在 methods course 后，让学生应用 citation 和 evidence skills。

questions.prompt: What problem can rigid sequences create?
options: They may complicate plans for transfer or major-change students.
They make introductory classes too short.
They remove advanced courses from the catalog.
They require every student to study economics.
questions.explanationZh: 文章指出 rigid sequences 会在学生转专业或转学时造成问题。

sections.title: Academic Passage: Collaboration and Learning
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术说明文，回答主旨、推断、细节和结构题。
sections.passage: Collaboration is often praised because it exposes students to different ideas, but the benefits are not automatic. Productive groups usually establish roles, deadlines, and methods for resolving disagreement. Without these structures, one student may complete most of the work while others participate only superficially. Research on cooperative learning suggests that the strongest groups combine individual accountability with shared goals. Each member is responsible for a specific contribution, yet the final product requires the pieces to fit together. This arrangement encourages students to explain their reasoning because unclear work affects the entire group. Teachers can support collaboration by asking teams to submit brief progress reports. These reports do not need to be long; their value is that they make planning visible. When a team notices a delay early, it can adjust tasks before the problem becomes serious. Collaboration therefore works best when it is treated as a skill to be managed rather than a simple instruction to work together.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why collaboration needs structure to support learning
Why group projects should be eliminated
How teachers grade oral presentations
How students choose research topics
questions.explanationZh: 文章主旨是合作学习需要角色、期限、责任和目标等结构。

questions.prompt: What risk is mentioned when groups lack structure?

Students may avoid different ideas

questions.evidence: one student may complete most of the work

questions.prompt: Why is individual accountability useful?
options: It connects each member’s work to the final product
It prevents students from explaining ideas
It lets teachers avoid progress reports
It removes shared goals
questions.explanationZh: 个人责任和共同目标结合，能让每个人的贡献融入最终成果。

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of progress reports?

sections.title: Academic Passage: Sleep and Memory
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术说明文，回答主旨、推断、细节和结构题。
sections.passage: Sleep is sometimes treated as empty time, but cognitive research suggests that it plays an active role in learning. During sleep, the brain appears to reorganize information gathered during the day. This process may strengthen useful connections while weakening details that are less important. Studies of memory often compare people who sleep after learning a task with people who remain awake for the same amount of time. The sleeping group frequently performs better later, especially when the task involves patterns or sequences. Researchers are careful, however, not to describe sleep as a simple replacement for practice. A student who never studies cannot expect sleep to create knowledge. Instead, sleep seems to help the brain process material that has already been introduced. This finding has practical implications for students. Reviewing material over several days, with sleep between sessions, is usually more effective than staying awake all night before an exam.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Sleep can support learning after material has been studied
Sleep replaces the need for practice
Memory research rejects studying over several days
Students should study only at night
questions.explanationZh: 文章说明睡眠能帮助处理已学习材料，但不能替代学习。

questions.prompt: What do memory studies often compare?
options: People who sleep after learning and people who remain awake
Students in different majors
People who study in libraries and dorms
Tasks with no patterns
questions.explanationZh: 文中说研究常比较学习后睡觉的人和保持清醒的人。
questions.evidence: compare people who sleep ... with people who remain awake

questions.prompt: Why is sleep not a replacement for practice?

It only helps physical exercise
It weakens all memory connections
It prevents students from reviewing
questions.explanationZh: 作者指出不学习就不能期待睡眠创造知识。
questions.evidence: cannot expect sleep to create knowledge

questions.explanationZh: empty time 指人们误以为睡眠是不活动、无产出的时间。

sections.title: Academic Passage: Museums and Interpretation
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术说明文，回答主旨、推断、细节和结构题。
sections.passage: Museums do more than store objects. They also create interpretations by selecting, arranging, and explaining what visitors see. A single artifact can support several stories depending on the questions curators ask. For instance, a ceramic bowl might be presented as evidence of artistic style, trade networks, household routines, or technological skill. Labels and room design guide visitors toward one interpretation, but they cannot include every possible meaning. This limitation has led many museums to experiment with multiple voices. Some exhibitions include comments from community members, artists, scientists, or students. These additions do not remove the curator’s role; rather, they show that objects can be understood from more than one perspective. The challenge is balance. Too little explanation may leave visitors confused, while too much may prevent them from observing carefully. Effective interpretation therefore gives enough context to support understanding while still leaving space for curiosity.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Museums shape interpretation while leaving room for multiple perspectives
Museums should stop using labels
Artifacts have only one correct meaning
Visitors should avoid curator explanations
questions.explanationZh: 全文说明博物馆通过选择和解释塑造意义，同时可纳入多种声音。

questions.prompt: Why is the ceramic bowl mentioned?
options: To show one object can support different interpretations
To describe the oldest museum object
To argue that art is more important than trade
To explain how bowls are repaired
questions.explanationZh: 陶碗例子说明同一物件可从艺术、贸易、日常、技术等角度解释。

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of including multiple voices?

To remove all curator responsibility
To make labels unnecessary
To prevent visitors from asking questions
questions.explanationZh: 多种声音展示物件可从多个角度理解。

questions.explanationZh: 结尾说解释要足够支持理解，又给好奇和观察留空间。

questions.explanationZh: 标签引导理解，但不可能包含所有意义。

sections.title: Academic Passage: Microclimates in Urban Areas
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术说明文，回答主旨、推断、细节和结构题。
sections.passage: A microclimate is a small area with climate conditions that differ from those nearby. In cities, microclimates can develop because surfaces absorb and release heat at different rates. Asphalt, brick, grass, and water do not respond to sunlight in the same way. A courtyard surrounded by buildings may remain warm after sunset, while a shaded garden may stay cooler during the afternoon. These differences matter because people often experience climate at the scale of streets and buildings rather than at the scale of regional forecasts. Urban planners use microclimate data to decide where to plant trees, place benches, or reduce paved surfaces. However, measuring microclimates is difficult. Conditions can change over a few meters or within a short period of time. A sensor placed in direct sunlight may record a different temperature from one placed under a tree. For this reason, researchers must document sensor location carefully and interpret local measurements with attention to surrounding materials.

questions.evidence: small area with climate conditions that differ

questions.prompt: Why are asphalt, brick, grass, and water mentioned?

sections.title: Academic Passage: Urban Transportation
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术说明文，回答主旨、推断、细节和结构题。
sections.passage: Research on urban transportation often begins with a simple campus problem, but useful explanations require more than quick observation. Students must decide what counts as evidence, how information will be collected, and whether a pattern might have more than one cause. For example, a change in student behavior may appear after a new policy is announced, yet the policy may not be the only explanation. Weather, timing, peer influence, or access to resources can also affect the result. Good researchers therefore compare several sources of information instead of relying on a single impression. They may combine short surveys with direct observation, interviews, or records kept by campus offices. Each source has limits. Surveys can reveal attitudes, but they depend on honest and careful answers. Observation can show behavior, but it may miss the reasons behind that behavior. Records may cover a long period, but they may have been collected for administrative rather than research purposes. The strongest projects acknowledge these limits and explain why the evidence still supports a reasonable conclusion.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Campus research requires careful evidence from more than one source
Student behavior can be explained by one cause in most cases
Surveys are always more reliable than observation
Administrative records should not be used in research
questions.explanationZh: 全文强调校园研究需要定义证据、比较多种信息来源，并说明局限。

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention weather, timing, and peer influence?

To list the only causes of student behavior
To argue that policy changes never matter
To explain how to design a weather station
questions.explanationZh: 这些因素说明行为变化可能不只由政策造成。

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what is one limitation of surveys?
options: They depend on honest and careful answers
They cannot ask about attitudes
They always take several years
They are collected only by administrators
questions.explanationZh: 文章明确说问卷依赖诚实、认真作答。
questions.evidence: depend on honest and careful answers

questions.prompt: What can observation show?
options: Behavior
All reasons behind behavior
Official budget records
Long-term administrative policy
questions.explanationZh: 文中说 observation can show behavior。

questions.prompt: What makes a project stronger, according to the passage?
options: Acknowledging limits while explaining why evidence supports a conclusion

Using only a single campus office record
Avoiding comparisons among information sources
questions.explanationZh: 结尾说强项目会承认证据局限，并解释为何仍支持合理结论。

sections.passage: Research on library space planning often begins with a simple campus problem, but useful explanations require more than quick observation. Students must decide what counts as evidence, how information will be collected, and whether a pattern might have more than one cause. For example, a change in student behavior may appear after a new policy is announced, yet the policy may not be the only explanation. Weather, timing, peer influence, or access to resources can also affect the result. Good researchers therefore compare several sources of information instead of relying on a single impression. They may combine short surveys with direct observation, interviews, or records kept by campus offices. Each source has limits. Surveys can reveal attitudes, but they depend on honest and careful answers. Observation can show behavior, but it may miss the reasons behind that behavior. Records may cover a long period, but they may have been collected for administrative rather than research purposes. The strongest projects acknowledge these limits and explain why the evidence still supports a reasonable conclusion.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Campus research requires careful evidence from more than one source
Student behavior can be explained by one cause in most cases
Surveys are always more reliable than observation
Administrative records should not be used in research
questions.explanationZh: 全文强调校园研究需要定义证据、比较多种信息来源，并说明局限。

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention weather, timing, and peer influence?
options: To show that a pattern may have several possible causes
To list the only causes of student behavior
To argue that policy changes never matter
To explain how to design a weather station
questions.explanationZh: 这些因素说明行为变化可能不只由政策造成。

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what is one limitation of surveys?
options: They depend on honest and careful answers
They cannot ask about attitudes
They always take several years
They are collected only by administrators
questions.explanationZh: 文章明确说问卷依赖诚实、认真作答。
questions.evidence: depend on honest and careful answers

questions.prompt: What can observation show?
options: Behavior
All reasons behind behavior
Official budget records
Long-term administrative policy
questions.explanationZh: 文中说 observation can show behavior。

questions.prompt: What makes a project stronger, according to the passage?
options: Acknowledging limits while explaining why evidence supports a conclusion
Ignoring sources that have any weakness
Using only a single campus office record
Avoiding comparisons among information sources
questions.explanationZh: 结尾说强项目会承认证据局限，并解释为何仍支持合理结论。

sections.title: Academic Passage: Habitat Observation
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读学术说明文，回答主旨、推断、细节和结构题。
sections.passage: Research on habitat observation often begins with a simple campus problem, but useful explanations require more than quick observation. Students must decide what counts as evidence, how information will be collected, and whether a pattern might have more than one cause. For example, a change in student behavior may appear after a new policy is announced, yet the policy may not be the only explanation. Weather, timing, peer influence, or access to resources can also affect the result. Good researchers therefore compare several sources of information instead of relying on a single impression. They may combine short surveys with direct observation, interviews, or records kept by campus offices. Each source has limits. Surveys can reveal attitudes, but they depend on honest and careful answers. Observation can show behavior, but it may miss the reasons behind that behavior. Records may cover a long period, but they may have been collected for administrative rather than research purposes. The strongest projects acknowledge these limits and explain why the evidence still supports a reasonable conclusion.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Campus research requires careful evidence from more than one source
Student behavior can be explained by one cause in most cases
Surveys are always more reliable than observation
Administrative records should not be used in research
questions.explanationZh: 全文强调校园研究需要定义证据、比较多种信息来源，并说明局限。

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention weather, timing, and peer influence?
options: To show that a pattern may have several possible causes
To list the only causes of student behavior
To argue that policy changes never matter
To explain how to design a weather station
questions.explanationZh: 这些因素说明行为变化可能不只由政策造成。

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what is one limitation of surveys?
options: They depend on honest and careful answers
They cannot ask about attitudes
They always take several years
They are collected only by administrators
questions.explanationZh: 文章明确说问卷依赖诚实、认真作答。
questions.evidence: depend on honest and careful answers

questions.prompt: What can observation show?
options: Behavior
All reasons behind behavior
Official budget records
Long-term administrative policy
questions.explanationZh: 文中说 observation can show behavior。

questions.prompt: What makes a project stronger, according to the passage?
options: Acknowledging limits while explaining why evidence supports a conclusion
Ignoring sources that have any weakness
Using only a single campus office record
Avoiding comparisons among information sources
questions.explanationZh: 结尾说强项目会承认证据局限，并解释为何仍支持合理结论。

sections.title: Academic Passage: Archives as Research Systems
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读文本，选择最佳答案。
sections.passage: In many university courses, archives are introduced as places where rare documents are stored. Yet archives are not simply storage rooms. They are systems for preserving evidence and making it usable. A letter, photograph, or map becomes more valuable for research when it is described accurately, connected to related materials, and protected from damage. Digitization has expanded this work. Scanned images allow students to examine documents without repeatedly touching fragile originals. However, digitization does not remove the need for human judgment. Someone must decide which details to describe, which names require clarification, and how uncertain dates should be recorded. If descriptions are too general, researchers may never find relevant items. If descriptions are too narrow, users may miss broader connections. Effective archival work therefore combines technical tools with careful interpretation. In classroom projects, these choices also teach students that evidence is not neutral until it is organized. A poorly labeled photograph can remain invisible even when the image file is technically available. Careful metadata, by contrast, lets students ask broader questions about change over time, community memory, and the limits of surviving records.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Archives become useful through preservation, description, and interpretation
Archives are mainly storage rooms for maps
Digitization eliminates the need for archivists
Students should avoid using original documents
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：systems for preserving evidence and making it usable。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。
questions.evidence: systems for preserving evidence and making it usable

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention scanned images?
options: To show how digitization can reduce handling of fragile originals
To argue that digital copies are always more accurate
To explain why all paper documents should be destroyed
To show that students dislike archives
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：without repeatedly touching fragile originals。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。

difficult to read
highly popular
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：protected from damage; fragile originals。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what can happen if descriptions are too general?
options: Researchers may not find relevant items
Documents become physically stronger
Maps lose their dates automatically
Students stop using digital tools
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：researchers may never find relevant items。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。
questions.evidence: researchers may never find relevant items

questions.prompt: What does the passage imply about archival decisions?

They can be made entirely by scanners
They are unrelated to research needs
They should avoid recording uncertainty
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：human judgment; uncertain dates should be recorded。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。
questions.evidence: human judgment; uncertain dates should be recorded

sections.title: Academic Passage: Desire Paths on Campus
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读文本，选择最佳答案。
sections.passage: Campus planners often study how students move through outdoor spaces before changing walkways or seating areas. A path that looks efficient on a map may not match the route students actually take between classes. For this reason, planners sometimes observe informal trails across lawns. These trails, often called desire paths, reveal repeated choices made by pedestrians. They do not automatically prove that a sidewalk should be built in the same place. A trail may cross a sensitive planting area, create drainage problems, or conflict with accessibility requirements. Still, ignoring such patterns can lead to designs that people avoid. Good planning treats observed behavior as one source of evidence. It combines that evidence with safety, environmental, and accessibility considerations before a final design is selected. For example, a route that saves two minutes for most students might be difficult for wheelchair users or might damage soil after heavy rain. Planners therefore treat the visible trail as a question rather than an answer. They ask why the route exists, who uses it, and what costs a permanent walkway might create. The result is usually a compromise.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How planners use observed walking patterns as one kind of evidence
Why students should be punished for walking on lawns
Why maps are more reliable than observation
How campuses can remove all outdoor seating
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：observed behavior as one source of evidence。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。
questions.evidence: observed behavior as one source of evidence

questions.prompt: The phrase "desire paths" refers to
options: informal trails created by repeated pedestrian choices
official roads designed for cars
areas where plants are watered
maps used in planning meetings
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：informal trails across lawns... reveal repeated choices。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。
questions.evidence: informal trails across lawns... reveal repeated choices

questions.prompt: Why might planners decide not to build a sidewalk on a desire path?
options: The trail may create environmental or accessibility problems
Students never use desire paths between classes
Maps cannot show outdoor spaces
Sidewalks are not allowed near classrooms
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：sensitive planting area, drainage problems, or accessibility requirements。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。
questions.evidence: sensitive planting area, drainage problems, or accessibility requirements

questions.prompt: What is the author’s attitude toward ignoring observed walking patterns?
options: It can produce designs that people avoid
It is always necessary for safety
It is the best way to protect lawns
It makes planning faster and more accurate
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：ignoring such patterns can lead to designs that people avoid。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。
questions.evidence: ignoring such patterns can lead to designs that people avoid

questions.prompt: Which approach is most consistent with the passage?

questions.evidence: combines that evidence with safety, environmental, and accessibility considerations

sections.title: Academic Passage: Peer Tutoring
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读文本，选择最佳答案。
sections.passage: Peer tutoring is often described as a way for students to receive quick answers, but its main value is usually different. A tutor does not simply correct a paper or solve a problem while the student watches. Instead, effective tutoring makes the student explain choices, test alternatives, and notice patterns of error. This process can be slower than receiving a direct answer, yet it builds independence. For example, a writing tutor may ask why a paragraph begins with a particular sentence. The question encourages the writer to think about organization rather than merely replace one sentence with another. Tutors also benefit from the exchange because explaining a concept often clarifies their own understanding. For this reason, peer tutoring is best understood as a guided conversation rather than a repair service. This distinction matters because students sometimes leave tutoring disappointed if they expected editing rather than learning. When expectations are clear, a session can focus on transferable strategies: reading the assignment carefully, identifying the point of confusion, and practicing a decision the student can repeat later. The conversation is successful when the learner can explain the next step independently.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Peer tutoring builds independence through guided conversation
Peer tutors should simply correct every error
Tutoring works only for writing courses
Students learn best by watching silently
questions.explanationZh: 正确答案对应原文证据：guided conversation rather than a repair service。其他选项要么过度推断，要么与文本细节不符。
questions.evidence: guided conversation rather than a repair service

sections.title: Academic Passage: Urban Habitat Patches
sections.instructionsZh: 阅读文本，选择最佳答案。
sections.passage: Urban ecologists increasingly study small habitat patches such as roadside plantings, vacant lots, and community gardens. Earlier research often treated these spaces as too disturbed to support meaningful biodiversity. More recent studies suggest a more complex picture. Although a single patch may contain only a limited number of species, a network of patches can support movement, feeding, and reproduction across a city. The value of such spaces depends partly on connectivity. A garden surrounded by pavement may function differently from one located near trees, drainage channels, or other vegetated areas. However, connectivity is not the only factor. Management choices, including mowing frequency and pesticide use, can alter habitat quality even when two sites appear similar on a map. Urban habitat studies therefore require both spatial analysis and close observation of local conditions. This combination is especially important because urban sites change quickly. A garden that supports insects in spring may become less useful after summer mowing, while a narrow strip of vegetation may become valuable if it links two larger patches. Researchers must therefore avoid treating a single observation as a permanent description of ecological value.
